Narrative:
On June 8, 2026, the customer called Kiaâs Customer Care Center to complain about a  Kia dealer that had been nonresponsive to the customerâs requests for warranty related information. During that discussion, the customer reported she been in an accident involving her 2026MY Kia Carnival on March [XXX], 2026 and that the vehicle caused her to swerve into a bridge.  The customer stated that âas soon as I got off the freeway [XXX], I put on the cruise control and I put on the lane assist, and while I was driving something took over the wheel, like the electrical system took over or something, the wheel spun out of control. I tried to do from the right lane into the left, and something took control of the car, and it crashed into the bridge.â The customer stated that she was traveling 75mph.  As a result of the crash, the customer reported she suffered from a concussion and had pain in her chest, abdomen and stomach and was transported directly to the hospital. The customer stated that Smart Cruise Control, Lane Following Assist and Highway Driving Assist were on while âdriving on [XXX] and on local streets.â The customer reported the vehicle had been repaired by a third-party facility.  The customer requested that Kia pay off her vehicle.
The subject Kia Carnival is equipped with Lane Following Assist (âLFAâ), Smart Cruise Control (âSCCâ) and Highway Driving Assist (HDA). LFA is a supplemental system that helps to detect lane markings and/or vehicles on the road and assists the driver's steering to help keep the vehicle between lanes. SCC is a supplemental system that detects a vehicle ahead and can help maintain the distance from the vehicle ahead and the set speed. The functionality of LFA and SCC are contingent upon several outside factors, and it is always the responsibility of the driver to safely steer the vehicle, to maintain the vehicle in its lane, and to maintain the vehicleâs speed. HDA is a supplemental system that helps maintain a set distance and speed from the vehicle ahead when driving on certain highways and helps keep the vehicle between lanes while driving. In addition, HDA may not be able to recognize all traffic situations and may not detect possible collisions due to limitations of the system.

Narrative:
The driver of a 2026 Ford F-150 reported that they were traveling westbound on [XXX], just before the [XXX] intersection (near Livonia, Michigan) on May [XXX] 2026 at approximately 9:45 p.m. The Ford driver reported that they were traveling with BlueCruise engaged at a very slow speed or not moving at all in stop and go traffic, when they were struck from behind by another vehicle, which the police estimated was traveling at approximately 60 mph. The Ford driver reported that there were no skid marks and that the other vehicle did not appear to brake prior to the collision. 
The Ford driver reported that the driver of the other vehicle smelled of marijuana when he got out of his vehicle and that he admitted to the police that he had been at "looking at his phone" prior to the collision. The Ford driver reported that airbags on the other vehicle deployed and that the person in other vehicle was injured and left the scene in an ambulance.  The [XXX] police department responded to the accident. 
Ford has confirmed that the Ford F-150 is equipped with Ford Co-Pilot360 Assist 2.0, which includes Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC), Lane Centering Assist (LCA), and BlueCruise. Ford also has obtained telematics data showing that, on the date and at the time of the collision: 1) the Ford vehicle was traveling with BlueCruise engaged and was decelerating to a pre-collision speed of approximately 8 mph; 2) the BlueCruise feature disengaged upon impact; 3) the collision triggered a fuel-cutoff event; and 4) post-collision braking engaged, bringing the vehicle to a complete stop. Ford's investigation continues.

Narrative:
From original report (July 25, 2025):
Entries in this report are based on unconfirmed allegations:

On July [XXX], 2025, Toyota received an initial contact from the driver of a rental vehicle involved in the crash. The driver described that cruise control was engaged with lane trace assist/lane departure assist and pre-collision system turned on leading up to the crash event. The driver alleged that while driving on a highway, the lane trace assist/lane departure assist failed to engage and keep vehicle within lanes, and the PCS failed to detect the median of the freeway and slow down the vehicle before impact. The driver stated he noticed twice before the incident the lane trace assist/lane departure would keep the vehicle in the lane. The driver stated the third time, the vehicle drifted out of the lane, and the driver side of the vehicle impacted the freeway median. The driver alleged the driver assistance features did not function properly and failed to prevent the accident.

As a result of the crash, the driver alleged that: the airbags deployed; there was significant damage to the front driver's side of the vehicle, including the wheel, tire, bumper, and fender; one of the five occupants seated in the left rear position sustained injuries to her left rib cage that required emergency room treatment (seat belt use unknown); and the vehicle was towed from the scene and deemed a total loss. 

The driver stated the California Highway Patrol responded to the crash and provided report numbers "NCIC - 9650".

From Update Report (September 15, 2025): 
On August 28, 2025, Toyota completed vehicle inspection field report related to the crash event and obtained the Event Data Recorder (EDR) record. The record indicated that the vehicle's Dynamic Radar Cruise Control (DRCC) was engaged in the recorded moments leading up to the crash event. Toyota is investigating the Lane Tracing Assist (LTA) engagement status, and the timing of driver inputs related to steering, acceleration, and braking. This information will be provided in a future update. Minor revisions to information in the report fields above were made based on factual information obtained from the field report and EDR record.

From Update Report (May 15, 2026): 
On May 12, 2026, following review of the available vehicle data, Toyota determined the LTA function was not active in the recorded moments (8 seconds) leading up to the crash, therefore the vehicle was not in an ADAS L2 state.  This will be the final update for this incident.

Narrative:
For the initial 5-Day report, see [XXX]


Update Report: 
Toyota completed an assessment of the crash event following review of all currently available data, i.e. field report, PCS FFD, and publicly available map information.  The vehicle was traveling east on [XXX] near the Meadow Vista, CA exit, 1hr 44min into the trip, when the crash event occurred.  In the moments leading up to the crash the vehicle was in an ADAS L2 state, with Lane Tracing Assist (LTA) and Dynamic Radar Cruise Control (DRCC) functions engaged and travelling at 82mph.  During a left hand curve, the vehicle was centered in the #1 lane (4.65 sec prior to the crash), when the driver induced a steering input (1.7Nm) to the right (3.15 sec prior to the crash).  The vehicle crossed the lane line  into the #2 lane which caused the LTA feature to cancel and disengage (2.40 sec prior to the crash).  The vehicle departed the #2 lane to the right shoulder (1.05 sec prior to the crash).  The vehicle continued drifting to the right without driver steering input before the right side tires exited the pavement and the car pulled to the right, at which the driver applied left steering input (0.30sec).  The vehicle went up the slope to the right side and moved left back into the roadway ending the crash event.  Based on this data, the initial driver input caused the vehicle to exit the lane, which caused the ADAS L2 to disengage, and the driver did not make steering inputs to attempt to regain control of the vehicle until the tires went off the shoulder, 0.30sec prior to the crash.  Also, it was observed that the customer installed a windshield decal (banner style at top) that was partially obstructing the forward camera; this can affect the ability of the camera to recognize lanes.  This is warned against in the owner's manual which states, "Warning: To prevent malfunction of the front camera: do not attach stickers (including transparent stickers) or other items to the area of the windshield in front of the front camera"  (The OM has an illustration of the windshield with shaded area representing area to avoid).  This will be the final update for this incident.

Narrative:
Initial report:
Entries in this report are based on unconfirmed allegations:

On March [XXX], 2026, Toyota received an initial contact from the owner and driver of a vehicle involved in the crash.  The owner described that leading up to the crash event, the owner was operating the vehicle in cruise control and lane assist mode, and alleged that while driving on a highway in the first lane, the vehicle suddenly veered across multiple lanes into a ditch and struck a tree.  The owner alleged failures in the cruise control, lane assist, brakes, steering, and pre-collision systems.  As a result of the crash, the airbags deployed, and the vehicle was deemed a total loss.  The owner was accompanied by his wife and two other family members.  The owner alleged that due to the airbag deployment, the owner received bruises on both legs and swelling and left arm burned by air bags, and the wife has shoulder bruises and swelling and a bruise below her face.  The owner stated their seat belt was used, the other three passengers seat belt usage is unknown.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

On May 4, 2026, Toyota completed review of a vehicle inspection field report related to the crash event and obtained the Event Data Recorder (EDR) record and PCS Freeze Frame Data (PCS FFD) from the crash event.  Additionally, a crash report from the  [XXX] was provided. 

Based on the [XXX] Crash Report (26-060417), the weather conditions were rainy with dusk lighting conditions and the roadway was wet with some standing water.  The speed limit of the roadway (I-10) for this location is 70 mph.  A citation was given related to speeding (2865HCLA36:32:64 General Speed Law) and the speed related circumstances are coded "too fast for conditions."

A review of the PCS FFD indicated the pre-crash speed as 77.6 mph and verified that dynamic radar cruise control (DRCC) and Lane Tracing Assist (LTA) were engaged in the recorded moments leading to the crash event.  While traveling straight and approaching an overpass, the vehicle began to sway and LTA turned off.  The driver made steering inputs and applied the brakes, but control of the vehicle was lost.  The subject vehicle, originally positioned in lane 1, swerved and after the overpass slid off the road to the right into the grass and tree line before coming to a stop.  

As a result of the crash, the Driver Airbag and Driver Knee Airbag deployed, and the vehicle was declared a total loss with damage to the Front, Front Right, Front Left, Left, and Rear Left sides.  All passengers, positioned in the second and third row seats were buckled.  

Based on the available information, it is suspected that the road speed traveled and road conditions led the vehicle to hydroplane and the driver's loss of control.  The LTA and DRCC were operated in adverse environmental conditions contrary to owner's manual warnings.  This will be the final update for this incident.

Narrative:
March [XXX], 2026, Toyota received a call from the subject vehicle owner that he "was involved in an incident where the adaptive cruise control (DRCC) made the vehicle veer off the road, causing damages (unknown) and now the vehicle was deemed a total loss". The driver stated as a result of the crash there were no injuries and that "all airbags deployed except the seat airbags". The driver requested Toyota to document the claim and investigate the vehicle. An incident location was not provided at this time.

Toyota reviewed a field report dated April 30, 2026, documenting the inspection of the subject vehicle. The report provided additional details of the crash event, and included EDR, Vehicle Control History (VCH) and PCS Freeze Frame Data (PCS FFD). Based on the engagement status of ADAS L2 in moments leading up to the crash, this report is now being made. 

The field report indicated that the vehicle was used in motorsport events and had been highly modified. The field report provides details about the incident as told by the customer, who indicated he was driving home with his girlfriend (RF seat), travelling E/B on [XXX] at about 70 mph when the accident occurred, about150 miles into the drive. He said that despite owning and driving his vehicle for about 24,000 miles he had not yet used his ADAS-related features, so he wanted to try them on this trip since he had a long drive home. He first wanted to know what would happen if he took his hands off the steering wheel and he found out the vehicle provided him a driver warning until he regrasped the steering wheel and then the warning would turn off. The customer said he was around the Meadow Vista, CA exit when he tried the DRCC function on his vehicle. He said there were no other vehicles around him, so the system was essentially just on DRCC. He said "everything seemed normal" initially. Then as his vehicle began the left curve in the roadway the vehicle's lane assist "just cut off." He said the vehicle "straighten the wheel in the turn" on its own and this "caught [him] off guard". He said he did not react "in time to cut back left". He said his vehicle drove over the rumble strips on the side of the road edge and then "the road drops 2" into a rain gutter with a 10ÃÂ° grade and 3' width". He said his RF tire went off the edge and his car pulled toward the side of the mountain which has a shale rock face. He said his vehicle "went up the hillside, almost to 90ÃÂ° to the road" and he was surprised and thankful his car did not flip onto its side. The customer said when the vehicle momentum brought it down off the side of the mountain, the vehicle cut diagonally to the left and across the traffic lanes where it finally came to rest diagonally across the #1 and #2 lanes of traffic. 

As a result of the crash, the airbags deployed, and the vehicle was deemed a total loss. The driver alleged that due to the crash, he suffered a gash across his left knee, and the girlfriend was sore due to the airbag deployment and vehicle jolting. The driver stated that all occupants were wearing seat belts. A preliminary review of the VCH and PCS FFD indicated that ADAS L2 was engaged in the recorded moments prior to the crash. The vehicle was traveling 82 mph prior to the event. 

Toyota is investigating the Lane Tracing Assist (LTA) engagement status, and the driver inputs related to steering, acceleration, and braking. This information will be provided in a future update.

Narrative:
On April [XXX], 2026, the customer called Kiaâs Customer Care Center to report that her 2025MY Kia K4 was involved in an incident, the airbags did not deploy, and the cruise control allegedly âfailed.â Specifically, the customer indicated that she was asleep and her husband was driving the vehicle when âthe cruise control was set for safe driving and it failed,â the vehicle in front of them abruptly stopped, and the brakes did not engage. The customer added that her husband âhad to fight the steering wheel to be able to jerk the wheelâ and the âsteering assist was the only thing that worked but it was working against us.â The customer reported that the police had told them they were not maintaining a safe distance. Narrative:
On April 8, 2026, Ford was served with a search warrant requesting specific data for a 2025 Explorer (VIN: [XXX]) regarding an incident on March [XXX], 2026. A preliminary review of connected vehicle telemetry data did not yield significant data related to the event. On April 9, Ford contacted the [XXX], Indiana Sheriff's Office to discuss the warrant.

On April 10th, Ford had a follow-up phone conversation with the [XXX], during which the lead investigator stated that while traveling on northbound [XXX] near mile marker 107.5, the driver of a 2025 Explorer alleged that the vehicle initiated an unintended lane change.  The driver reportedly attempted to overcome the systems but was unable to do so, causing the vehicle to leave the roadway and proceed into the median embankment, resulting in a rollover. The lead investigator further stated that the vehicle came to rest on its roof but that no airbags were deployed. He reported that the vehicle had six occupants, four of whom sustained injuries and were transported to a hospital for treatment. 

Ford has obtained the police accident report and the Event Data Recorder (EDR) download. Preliminary EDR analysis indicates that Cruise Control was active but was subsequently disengaged by a driver brake application. The available EDR data does not confirm whether any Level 2 (L2) Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS) were active immediately preceding the incident. Analysis of the EDR readout indicates that the turn signal status remained inactive leading up to the incident. Furthermore, witness statements in the police report describe the vehicle swerving and drifting gradually into the highway median.

Ford has verified that the vehicle is equipped with Ford Co-Pilot360Active 2.0, which includes Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC), Lane Centering Assist (LCA), and BlueCruise version 1.2 technology. BlueCruise version 1.2 does not support automatic system-initiated lane changes; this version only supports lane changes initiated by the driver. Ford will continue to investigate whether L2 features were active at the time of the incident.  Ford remains in communication with the police investigator and will continue to support the ongoing police accident investigation.

Narrative: On April [XXX], 2026, log data shows that the subject vehicle started a trip at approximately 04:40 AM ET and continued driving for 14 hours, with a few brief stops, up until 18:31 PM ET, the time of the collision. Immediately before the collision, the subject vehicle was traveling approximately 71 mph eastbound on the [XXX] Massachusetts with Universal Hands Free (âUHFâ) engaged. The driver actuated the turn signal stalk a half-detent (which activates the turn signal three times and disables UHF steering control) allowing the driver to control the lane change. Here, it appears the driver was attempting a lane change into the right lane.  The driver started the attempted lane change with their hands on the steering wheel but before the lane change was complete and the UHF reengagement parameters could be met for the new lane, the driverâs hands came off the steering wheel (18:31:44 PM). With UHF lateral control deactivated, the driver responsible for lateral control, and their hands off the steering wheel, the vehicle proceeded according to road curvature, drifting back toward the left lane and ultimately beyond it. A few seconds after the driverâs hands come off the wheel the driver monitoring system data shows that the driverâs gaze went from looking âon roadâ to âdownâ for a couple seconds and then to âeyes closedâ for approximately 4 seconds. This data indicates the driver fell asleep at the wheel. At 18:51:53 PM ET the vehicle goes off the left side of the roadway, the driverâs hands go back on the steering wheel, and their eyes gaze is âon roadâ, but at this point vehicle contact with the guardrail is imminent. The data indicates that the vehicle responded appropriately to all inputs. The driver failing to maintain control of the vehicle was the cause of the collision.

Narrative:
On March [XXX], 2026, at approximately 7:23 p.m., a 2025 Ford F-150 was traveling westbound on [XXX] near mile marker [XXX] in Broadview Heights, Ohio. The driver alleges he was driving in the left-most lane with BlueCruise engaged. A semi-truck traveling in the middle lane began to cross over into the left-most lane where the F-150 was traveling. As the semi-truck began to enter the left-most lane, the F-150 driver received a warning to "Keep hands on steering wheel", took control of the vehicle, and veered left to avoid the semi-truck, resulting in an impact with a concrete jersey barrier. The impact damaged the left side of the F-150 and deployed the airbags. The driver stated he had prior issues with the seatbelt buckle warning chime and indicator, but that the seatbelt was buckled at the time of the accident. The vehicle was towed from the scene; no injuries were reported. 
Ford's investigation confirmed the F-150 is equipped with Co-Pilot 360 Assist 2.0, including Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC), Lane Centering Assist (LCA), and BlueCruise. 
Telemetry connected vehicle data was retrieved, and Ford's initial analysis shows: (1) BlueCruise was engaged within 30 seconds prior to the incident with prerequisite seatbelt status detected as buckled; (2) the vehicle displayed the warning "Keep hands on steering wheel" due to detection of seatbelt unbuckle status; (3) the system detected the driver hands on the wheel, and a driver input steered the vehicle to the left, resulting in impact with the concrete barrier; (4) driver airbags and knee airbags deployed; and (5) the driver seatbelt status was recorded as "Unbelted" at the time of the incident.  Ford's investigation is ongoing.

Entries in this report are based on unconfirmed allegations:

On March [XXX], 2026, Toyota received an initial contact from the owner and driver of a vehicle involved in the crash. Narrative:
On March [XXX], 2026, at approximately 11:13 AM, a Lincoln Aviator was involved in an incident while traveling on [XXX] South near exit [XXX]. The driver alleged she was traveling at 78 mph with BlueCruise engaged when her leg contacted the steering wheel, causing the vehicle to steer and impact the center steel highway guardrail. According to the driver, airbags deployed, and a rear-row passenger was later treated at the hospital for a concussion.
Ford's investigation confirmed the Aviator is equipped with Co-Pilot 360 Drive 2.0, including Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC), Lane Centering Assist (LCA), and BlueCruise. Telemetry connected vehicle data was retrieved, and Ford's initial analysis shows: 1) BlueCruise was engaged at the time of the incident; 2) the vehicle was traveling at a pre-collision speed of 78 mph; 3) the vehicle experienced a non-system initiated leftward steer input of 39 degrees resulting in impact with the steel guardrail; 4) side and curtain airbags deployed; and 5) a rightward steer input of 94 degrees was recorded after the impact.  The initial rapid leftward angle change at a high vehicle speed (78 mph) far exceeds the steering input capabilities of the BlueCruise system. Ford's investigation is ongoing.

Narrative:
On January 2, 2026, the customer called Kia's Customer Care Center regarding his 2025MY Kia K4, indicating that, on December [XXX], 2025, he was in "an accident because cruise control would not disengage." The customer also indicated that Lane Following Assist was also on. He alleged that while driving, he saw a "red light ahead and I had cruise control on." He attempted to "press on the brakes," but "it didn't take off cruise control even after turning it off." The subject K4 came into contact with a white Ford SUV and the customer reported both drivers sustained injuries.

Kia subsequently requested and received from the customer photos and the Arizona Traffic Ticket and Complaint given to the customer.

The subject Kia K4 is equipped with Lane Following Assist ("LFA") and Smart Cruise Control ("SCC"). LFA is a supplemental system that helps to detect lane markings and/or vehicles on the road and assists the driver's steering to help keep the vehicle between lanes. SCC is a supplemental system that detects a vehicle ahead and can help maintain the distance from the vehicle ahead and the set speed. The functionality of LFA and SCC are contingent upon several outside factors, and it is always the responsibility of the driver to safely steer the vehicle, to maintain the vehicle in its lane, and to maintain the vehicle's speed. 

Kia is working with the customer to arrange an inspection of the vehicle. 

Kia has no further information at this time.

**Monthly Update 3/16/2026**
After attempting to arrange an inspection of the vehicle, KA learned that the vehicle had been sold and would not be available for inspection. KA has no further information.

Narrative:
Monthly update on report [XXX] (reported on Feb [XXX], 2026):
Ford obtained the police report relating to this incident. According to the police report, the driver stated that, while utilizing the hands-free driving system, he took his eyes off the road and reached into the rear of the vehicle to retrieve a hat. While the driver was reaching into the rear of the vehicle, it drifted left toward the guardrail. The driver attempted to correct but lost control, crashing into the guardrail. 

Ford also obtained physical access to the vehicle and was able to extract EDR data from the Restraints Control Module. This data shows: 1) that the vehicle was traveling at a pre-collision speed of 69 mph; 2) that approximately one second prior to impacting the barrier the steering wheel angle registered a leftward input of 40 degrees; and 3) that this leftward steering input occurred rapidly, in less than half a second. This extreme (40 degrees) and rapid (<0.5 second) steering angle change at high vehicle speed (69 mph) far exceeds the steering input capabilities of the BlueCruise system. 

The police report and the EDR data therefore confirm that the incident was caused by abrupt manual steering wheel input, most likely from inadvertent contact with the steering wheel while the driver was turning and reaching into the rear of the vehicle.

Narrative:
From initial 5-Day report (October 23, 2025):
Entries in this report are based on unconfirmed allegations:

On October [XXX], 2025, Toyota received an initial contact from a rental car company, alleging that a driver of a rental vehicle was involved in a crash, and the driver claimed that a malfunction with the adaptive cruise control setting caused the crash.  The driver described that, while driving on a rural highway with adaptive cruise control engaged and passing a turnoff on the left side, a malfunction warning appeared that said "adaptive cruise control malfunction," and the vehicle pulled hard to the left and the steering locked.  As the driver attempted to straighten the vehicle, it impacted the center cable divider.  The driver alleged the steering wheel then released and caused the car to steer across two lanes of traffic to the right and the vehicle rolled.  As a result of the crash, the driver alleged that: the curtain airbags deployed; there was damage to the left side and top of vehicle including a broken driver's window and rear windscreen; the driver suffered from a concussion and an occupants left arm was cut, both treated at an emergency room; the vehicle was towed away from the scene. 

The rental company stated the [XXX] responded to the crash and provided report numbers "[XXX]".

On October 21, 2025, Toyota communicated directly with the driver of the vehicle. The driver provided additional information indicating that Lane Trace Assist feature was engaged at the time of the incident.

~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Update report (January 13, 2026): 
On January 9, 2025, Toyota completed vehicle inspection field report related to the crash event and obtained the Event Data Recorder (EDR) record and PCS Freeze Frame Data (PCS FFD) from the crash event. The PCS FFD showed that dynamic radar cruise control (DRCC) was engaged in the recorded moments leading to the crash event. Toyota is investigating the Lane Tracing Assist (LTA) engagement status, and the timing of driver inputs related to steering, acceleration, and braking. This information will be provided in a future update. Minor revisions to information in the report fields above were made based on factual information obtained from the field report and EDR record.

~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Update report (January 14, 2026):
No materially new information is available at this time. Submitting update report to correct an error in [XXX].

~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Update report (February 13, 2026):
On February 12, 2025, Toyota completed their assessment of the Lane Tracing Assist (LTA) engagement status, and the timing of the driver inputs related to steering, acceleration, and braking. The PCS Freeze Frame Data (PCS FFD) consistent with the crash event was reviewed. The Lane Tracing Assist (LTA) turned off approximately two seconds prior to left steering inputs followed by the vehicle drifting off the roadway to the left. Right steering input began and brakes were applied briefly disabling the Dynamic Radar Cruise Control (DRCC). The right steering inputs continued as the vehicle began to roll over. Based on this information, Toyota determined that the Level 2 ADAS was not engaged in time leading up to the crash. Therefore, this will be the final update for this incident.

Narrative:
From initial 5-Day report (October 23, 2025):
Entries in this report are based on unconfirmed allegations:

On October 17, 2025, Toyota received an initial contact from a rental car company, alleging that a driver of a rental vehicle was involved in a crash, and the driver claimed that a malfunction with the adaptive cruise control setting caused the crash.  The driver described that, while driving on a rural highway with adaptive cruise control engaged and passing a turnoff on the left side, a malfunction warning appeared that said "adaptive cruise control malfunction," and the vehicle pulled hard to the left and the steering locked.  As the driver attempted to straighten the vehicle, it impacted the center cable divider.  The driver alleged the steering wheel then released and caused the car to steer across two lanes of traffic to the right and the vehicle rolled.  As a result of the crash, the driver alleged that: the curtain airbags deployed there was damage to the left side and top of vehicle including a broken driver's window and rear windscreen; the driver suffered from a concussion and an occupants left arm was cut, both treated at an emergency room; the vehicle was towed away from the scene. 

The rental company stated the [XXX] responded to the crash and provided report numbers "[XXX]".

On October 21, 2025, Toyota communicated directly with the driver of the vehicle. The driver provided additional information indicating that Lane Trace Assist feature was engaged at the time of the incident.

~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Update report (January 13, 2026): 
On January 9, 2025, Toyota completed vehicle inspection field report related to the crash event and obtained the Event Data Recorder (EDR) record and PCS Freeze Frame Data (PCS FFD) from the crash event. The PCS FFD showed that dynamic radar cruise control (DRCC) was engaged in the recorded moments leading to the crash event. Toyota is investigating the Lane Tracing Assist (LTA) engagement status, and the timing of driver inputs related to steering, acceleration, and braking. This information will be provided in a future update. Minor revisions to information in the report fields above were made based on factual information obtained from the field report and EDR record.

~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Update report (January 14, 2026):
No materially new information is available at this time. Submitting update report to correct an error in 1306-11959-2.

Narrative:
Entries in this report are based on unconfirmed allegations:
On November [XXX], 2025, Toyota received initial contact from the mother, calling on behalf of her daughter. She alleged her daughter was involved in an accident where the cruise control on her 2024 Toyota Corolla got stuck and caused her to crash into a ditch. The caller believes there may have been a malfunction with the cruise control. As a result of the crash, the caller alleged that: the airbag deployed there were no serious injuries; and the vehicle was deemed a total loss by the insurance company. 

On January 9, 2026, Toyota completed a field report based on inspection of the subject vehicle. The field report contained EDR and PCS Freeze Frame Data (PCS FFD) from the crash event. The PCS FFD showed that dynamic radar cruise control (DRCC) and lane trace assist (LTA) were engaged in the recorded moments leading to the crash event. Based on the engagement status of level 2 ADAS in moments leading up to the crash, this report is now being made. 

The EDR data and PCS FFD confirmed the driver did not apply the brakes until 1.5sec before the airbag deployment. After the brake switch became ON, cruise control and lane centering turned off appropriately, and deceleration was observed consistent with the brake application. The loss of control experienced by the driver and subsequent damage are likely caused by the road speed traveled (50mph in a 35mph speed zone approaching a T-intersection controlled by a stop sign at the intersection) and timing of brake application (1.5 sec before airbag deployment) prior to going through the intersection and off the road. Therefore, this will be the final update for this incident.

Narrative:
On January [XXX], 2026, the customer called Kia's Customer Care Center regarding his 2025MY Kia K4, indicating that, on December [XXX], 2025, he was in ?an accident because cruise control would not disengage. The customer also indicated that Lane Following Assist was also on. He alleged that while driving, he saw a red light ahead and I had cruise control on.He attempted to press on the brakes, but it didn't take off cruise control even after turning it off. The subject K4 came into contact with a white Ford SUV and the customer reported both drivers sustained injuries.

Kia subsequently requested and received from the customer photos and the Arizona Traffic Ticket and Complaint given to the customer.

The subject Kia K4 is equipped with Lane Following Assist (LFA) and Smart Cruise Control (SCC). LFA is a supplemental system that helps to detect lane markings and/or vehicles on the road and assists the driver's steering to help keep the vehicle between lanes. SCC is a supplemental system that detects a vehicle ahead and can help maintain the distance from the vehicle ahead and the set speed. The functionality of LFA and SCC are contingent upon several outside factors, and it is always the responsibility of the driver to safely steer the vehicle, to maintain the vehicle in its lane, and to maintain the vehicle's speed. 

Kia is working with the customer to arrange an inspection of the vehicle. 

Kia has no further information at this time.

Narrative:
On December [XXX], 2025, Ford received notification of an incident, involving an 2025 F-150.  The incident occurred on December [XXX], 2025. According to the driver's account, the vehicle was traveling northbound on [XXX], occupying the leftmost lane of a three-lane highway.  The driver reported that BlueCruise was engaged at the time. 
As the F-150 approached the on-ramp leading to [XXX], the driver observed that the vehicle immediately ahead had abruptly changed lanes to avoid stopped traffic. The driver stated that he took manual control of the vehicle, applied the brakes, and steered into the center lane.  While executing this maneuver, the F-150 was struck from behind by a trailing vehicle.  The impact propelled the F-150 forward, resulting in a second collision with the vehicle in front of the F-150. 

The F-150 driver reported that the driver of the vehicle that rear-ended the F-150 experienced an airbag deployment, sustained injuries, and was transported to the hospital. No other injuries or airbag deployments were reported as a result of the incident. 

Given the F-150 driver's assertion that BlueCruise was active prior to the incident, Ford conducted a search for connected vehicle data associated with the event.  Ford's analysis of this data confirms that ADAS L2 (BlueCruise) was engaged within 30 seconds before the collision and that BlueCruise disengaged when the driver pressed the brakes, approximately seven seconds before the F-150 was rear-ended.

Narrative:
On September [XXX], 2025, the customer called Kia's Customer Care Center regarding his 2025MY Kia K4, indicating that, in July he was in a car accident and the "lane assist" jerked to the left and hit a barrier." The customer reported that before this incident, he had "noticed it would start to jerk the wheel," but he "always had hands on [REDACTED, MAY CONTAIN CONFIDENTIAL BUSINESS INFORMATION] tried to correct it," but "it was semi-locked and wouldn't turn," resulting in the vehicle crashing into a cable barrier. The customer alleged "his left shoulder hurt". The customer requested an inspection of the vehicle.
The subject Kia K4 is equipped with Lane Following Assist ("LFA"), Lane Keeping Assist ("LKA"), and Smart Cruise Control ("SCC"). It is unclear which feature the customer is referring to by "lane assist." However, only LFA and SCC engaged at the same could qualify as a Level 2 ADAS feature under the proper conditions.

LFA is a supplemental system that helps to detect lane markings and/or vehicles on the road and assists the driver's steering to help keep the vehicle between lanes. The functionality of LFA is contingent upon several outside factors and it is always the responsibility of the driver to safely steer the vehicle and to maintain the vehicle in its lane. 

Kia has attempted to contact the customer on several occasions to obtain additional information to evaluate the incident but customer has not responded.

** Monthly Update Report 12/15/2025***
KA received the subject vehicle's ACU and downloaded the vehicle's EDR data, which indicates that Smart Cruise Control was not engaged during the 5-seconds prior to the crash and thus vehicle was not being operated at LVL 2. The vehicle was steered rapidly to the left while being driven at highway speeds and then an evasive maneuver was performed steering the vehicle to the right. This amount of steering input exceeds what Lane Keep Asist / Lane Follow Asist can apply.  No Advanced Driving Assistance Systems that provide steering inputs were found to have been a contributing or causal factor in the collision. This information, including documents (subject vehicle EDR report, photos, customer care case, etc.) were provided to ODI engineer, Joseph Teitelman on 12/5/2025 per his request.

The customer later visited an ER for pain and minor bruising.

The subject vehicle was traveling [REDACTED, MAY CONTAIN CONFIDENTIAL BUSINESS INFORMATION] in an area of road construction when a construction vehicle in front pulled to a stop. Per the owner's manual, if the speed difference with an identified object is greater than approximately 37 MPH (60 km/h), collisions cannot be avoided. Additionally, as also published in the owner's manual, Subaru does not recommend operating the vehicle with ACC or LCF engaged on roads with lane restrictions or tentative lanes due to construction work, etc.

Narrative:
According to the customer's statement, the subject vehicle was in the process of making a left turn at an intersection when the Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) and Lane Centering (LCF) both engaged without input from the driver. Upon engagement, the vehicle speed increased, and the steering wheel could not be turned leftward and the vehicle proceeded straight. An Illinois Traffic Crash Report stated that while attempting to make a left-hand turn, the driver continued to press the accelerator pedal and ran off the roadway onto private property. The vehicle struck 8-10 concrete landscaping bricks that were around a tree, causing disabling damage to the vehicle. There was no airbag deployment, and the vehicle was towed from the scene. The driver was transported to the hospital for observation for high blood pressure. The customer indicated that the ACC had not been in use that day.

Subaru inspected the subject vehicle on 10/07/2025. The vehicle was under repair and the front bumper cover, left headlight, left fender, and driver's door were removed. Three tires and the electric power steering (EPS) gearbox were replaced. In the interior, a genuine all season floor mat was secured to the retaining pins on the driver's floorboard. There were no abnormalities with the brake or accelerator pedal. SSM data indicated there were no DTCs before the collision and multiple DTCs were detected due to the crash and disassembly during repairs.

[REDACTED, MAY CONTAIN CONFIDENTIAL BUSINESS INFORMATION]

Based on the vehicle inspection and the data acquired, Subaru determined that the ACC and LCF had not been engaged on the day of the incident and there were no abnormalities with the vehicle's steering, accelerator or brakes. The data, further supported by the crash report, indicated the event experienced by the customer was likely due to operational error by the driver.

Narrative:
From initial 5-Day report (September 18, 2025):
Entries in this report are based on unconfirmed allegations:

On September [XXX], 2025, Toyota received an initial contact from the daughter of an owner/driver of a vehicle involved in the crash. The caller alleged that her father's car had veered to the left abruptly and caused a wreck while his hands were on the steering wheel while using cruise control and lane assist. The caller stated the vehicle went to the right side of the road and hit a dirt pile. The caller alleged the cruise control and lane assist features caused the event to occur. As a result of the crash, the caller alleged that: there is damage to the Grill, Front Bumper, and Driver's Side Mirror the driver was transported to the emergency room by the caller for body, neck, back and spinal soreness; and the vehicle was towed from the scene.

The driver stated the California Highway Patrol responded to the crash and provided report numbers "NCIC - 9625".

Update report (October 13, 2025): 
On September 26, 2025 Toyota completed a field report based on inspection of the subject vehicle. The field report contained EDR and PCS Freeze Frame Data (PCS FFD) from the crash event. The PCS FFD showed that dynamic radar cruise control (DRCC) was not engaged in the recorded moments leading to the crash event. Based on this information, Toyota determined that the Level 2 ADAS was not engaged in time leading up to the crash. Therefore, this will be the final update for this incident.

On September 29, 2025, the customer called Kias Customer Care Center regarding his 2025MY Kia K4, indicating that, in July he was in a car accident and the lane assist  jerked to the left and hit a barrier. The customer reported that before this incident, he had noticed it would start to jerk the wheel, but he always had hands on [REDACTED, MAY CONTAIN CONFIDENTIAL BUSINESS INFORMATION], so [REDACTED, MAY CONTAIN CONFIDENTIAL BUSINESS INFORMATION] could correct it easily. However, on this occasion, the wheel jerked to the left and [REDACTED, MAY CONTAIN CONFIDENTIAL BUSINESS INFORMATION] tried to correct it, but it was semi-locked and wouldnt turn, resulting in the vehicle crashing into a cable barrier. The customer alleged his left shoulder hurt.. The customer requested an inspection of the vehicle.
The subject Kia K4 is equipped with Lane Following Assist (LFA), Lane Keeping Assist (LKA), and Smart Cruise Control (SCC). It is unclear which feature the customer is referring to by lane assist. However, only LFA and SCC engaged at the same could qualify as a Level 2 ADAS feature under the proper conditions.LFA is a supplemental system that helps to detect lane markings and/or vehicles on the road and assists the driver's steering to help keep the vehicle between lanes. The functionality of LFA is contingent upon several outside factors and it is always the responsibility of the driver to safely steer the vehicle and to maintain the vehicle in its lane. 
Kia has attempted to contact the customer on several occasions to obtain additional information to evaluate the incident but customer has not responded.

Narrative:
Honda Customer Service contacted the customer to confirm the accident on [XXX], customer was driving on the[XXX] highway going eastbound, near [XXX]. He had ACC on, and it was sent to 78. There were no cars in front of him. Customer also had the lane keep assist on, which he recalls was green prior to the accident. The vehicle started pulling to the right and then collided with metal divider on the passenger side. Both the driver front airbag and driver knee airbags deployed. Honda asked how he was doing, which he informed me better. Honda asked if he had any injuries, which he said he had a scratch on his wrist. He did not know what scratched him. Honda asked about the damage to the vehicle, which he informed that the vehicle is not drivable. The right rear tire was flat, damage to the right side of fender, also right-side bumper in front, left front bumper as well, scratches on both passenger side front and rear door. Driver side, he mentioned that there was plastic piece that fell off, not sure what that was. Honda asked him about ACC, how long he had been using, and what speed it was sent for. Customer started he was using the ACC about half hour after he started his drive. It was sent to 78 MPH. Honda asked what the speed limit was for that area of highway, which he advised about 70MPH. Honda asked him about LKAS, how long has been using it, and if he recalled what the LKAS icon was prior to the accident. He also said that he turned LKAS on about half hour after he started his drive. He said prior the accident it was green. Honda asked if he recalls the conditions or markings on the road, which he could recall partially as it happened all so fast. Since it was a two-lane highway going east, his vehicle was on the right side. The left side he recalls the lines being white and dashed. Honda asked about the right side, but he could not recall, maybe solid white, but he was not sure. He mentioned there was a downside hill when he goes off the road, so road was higher than side of road. Honda asked around what time this occurred and weather conditions, which he advised around 12 PM, and it was sunny. Honda asked about the vehicle starting to go right if he recalls the steering conditions at the time. The vehicle was driving straight, then started going right. He mentioned he had his hand on the steering wheel. Honda asked once it started pulling right, did he apply steering correction, but he did not recall. Honda asked if he recalled putting his foot on the brake pedal, either right before hitting the railing or after. He does not think he did right before hit railing. He does recall after hitting the railing as that is probably what stopped the vehicle. Honda asked him if he recalls how long it was from vehicle starting to go right, to hitting the barrier, which he did not recall. Honda asked if metal barrier was solid, like one metal strip, or if there were two, with a gap in between, but he could not recall. After the accident, police did come out, and he received a $210 citation ticket, and a copy of the report.
NOTE: We recently let NHTSA's Office of Chief Council know about this past incident from 2023.

On Wednesday, January [XXX], 2023, at approximately 5:08 a.m., [XXX] was operating his Honda Civic traveling eastbound on [XXX], in rural Alice, Jim Wells
County, Texas.[XXX] is a four-lane highway with two (2) lanes running east and two (2) lanes running west that are divided by a grassy median. [XXX] was traveling in

At that time, a 2015 Mack Granite gin pole tractor (VIN 1.[XXX]) in combination with a commercial flatbed 1982 Lufkin Industries trailer (VIN [XXX]) was traveling ahead of [XXX] also in the eastbound inside lane. Narrative:
Honda customer service contacted and confirmed with the customer that the vehicle was in an accident and that the passenger side airbags deployed. The customer stated there were no injuries. I empathized with the customer and stated I was glad no injuries were sustained. I asked the customer to provide a brief description of the accident. The customer stated that ACC was set to 65 MPH and the distance was set to two bars. The customer stated there was only a line in the middle of the road since it was only a two-way road but can't remember the color of the line. The customer stated that suddenly the vehicle steered to the right, and he hit a fence. The customer can't remember if the LKAS indicator line were filled or blank but stated that it was hard to control the steering as it was steering itself. The customer stated he drove the vehicle away from that and when he got to a railroad crossing, he stepped on the brakes and the vehicle did not stop and he lost control of the vehicle. The customer stated that this is after the first incident of hitting the fence and ACC and LKAS were not on at this time because there were errors on the dash. The customer stated he contacted his insurance company, and they said this will be a total loss but hadn't not processed the total loss yet.

Narrative:
The subject MY 2023 Mercedes-Benz GLE 53 4MATIC is equipped with L2 ADAS DISTRONIC Plus with Active Steering Assist and was being driven under rainy conditions on September[XXX], 2025, at 11:42 am in the far-left lane on the [XXX] heading west in Houston, Texas.
 
The driver contacted the Mercedes-Benz USA ("MBUSA") Customer Care Center ("CCC") on September 10, 2025, and alleged that he was traveling under the 65 mph speed limit with his foot lightly on the accelerator pedal when a vehicle passed him on the right. The driver stated that "emergency braking" was applied but stated that he did not apply the "service brake." The driver lost control of the subject vehicle, it spun three times, struck the guard rail, and the airbags deployed. The driver stated that the subject vehicle "felt like it was hydroplaning". The driver alleged that the vehicle hydroplaned during an Active Brake Assist ("ABA") intervention in response to another vehicle cutting in front of him from the right lane. The driver stated that driver assistance features were active at the time of the incident but could not specify which ones.
 
On September 12, 2025, the driver informed MBUSA that DISTRONIC Plus with Active Steering Assist was allegedly activated at the time of the incident. The driver further alleged that "what made my vehicle lose control was a car tried to cut me from the right lane and my GLE automatically abruptly applied the emergency brake (AEB) and caused my vehicle to hydroplane." The driver stated he was traveling with two passengers in the back seat and all vehicle occupants were belted, and that the driver's front airbags and front and rear curtain airbags deployed. The driver did not report any injuries. The vehicle was totaled by the driver's insurance company and towed to a salvage yard.
In accordance with the requirements of the NHTSA ADAS SGO, MBUSA is submitting this 5-day incident report.
This narrative summary is based entirely on the customer's description and allegations. MBUSA has not confirmed or verified this account or any of the customer's allegations and has initiated further investigation into the alleged incident.

Narrative:
The information contained in this report is based on Nissan's understanding of the allegations received during Claimant's discussions with Nissan's Consumer Affairs representatives and Nissan's preliminary vehicle inspection.
On 8/21/25 claimant notified Nissan Consumer Affairs of an accident which reportedly occurred on 8/[XXX]/25.   The Claimant stated that the Claimant's son was driving the vehicle with "cruise assist" on with both hands on the wheel. Claimant stated the vehicle steered to the left on its own, colliding with oncoming traffic. Claimant was not hospitalized immediately after the incident but visited a physician on 8/20/25. Claimant states that the son sustained injuries to both hands and was concussed.
On 8/27/25, Nissan received the allegation that a curtain air bag had deployed during the incident and that claimant's son was hospitalized. 
On 9/4/25, Nissan inspected the vehicle. Primary vehicle damage was observed in the left front corner and proceeding down the left side of the vehicle. The left curtain, the driver, and the passenger knee airbags were deployed as a result of the impact. EDR data gathered from the vehicle did not support the claimant's allegations of the vehicle steering to the left proir to the incident.
On 9/10/25, Nissan requested a copy of the police report from the claimant. The claimant informed Nissan that they had already settled with the insurance company and that the investigation "would no longer be applicable."

Narrative:
From original report (July 25, 2025):
Entries in this report are based on unconfirmed allegations:
On July [XXX], 2025, Toyota received an initial contact from the driver of a rental vehicle involved in the crash. The driver described that cruise control was engaged with lane trace assist/lane departure assist and pre-collision system turned on leading up to the crash event. The driver alleged that while driving on a highway, the lane trace assist/lane departure assist failed to engage and keep vehicle within lanes, and the PCS failed to detect the median of the freeway and slow down the vehicle before impact. The driver stated he noticed twice before the incident the lane trace assist/lane departure would keep the vehicle in the lane. The driver stated the third time, the vehicle drifted out of the lane, and the driver side of the vehicle impacted the freeway median. The driver alleged the driver assistance features did not function properly and failed to prevent the accident.
As a result of the crash, the driver alleged that: the airbags deployed there was significant damage to the front driver's side of the vehicle, including the wheel, tire, bumper, and fender one of the five occupants seated in the left rear position sustained injuries to her left rib cage that required emergency room treatment (seat belt use unknown) and the vehicle was towed from the scene and deemed a total loss. 
The driver stated the California Highway Patrol responded to the crash and provided report numbers "NCIC - 9650".
From Update Report (September 15, 2025): 
On August 28, 2025, Toyota completed vehicle inspection field report related to the crash event and obtained the Event Data Recorder (EDR) record. The record indicated that the vehicle's Dynamic Radar Cruise Control (DRCC) was engaged in the recorded moments leading up to the crash event. Toyota is investigating the Lane Tracing Assist (LTA) engagement status, and the timing of driver inputs related to steering, acceleration, and braking. This information will be provided in a future update. Minor revisions to information in the report fields above were made based on factual information obtained from the field report and EDR record.

Narrative:
On September 8, 2025, we learned about a single-vehicle collision involving a [XXX] loaner vehicle. Specifically, on September [XXX], 2025, at approximately 8:13 a.m. MST, the subject vehicle was traveling on Interstate [XXX] near the [XXX] exit in Tempe, Arizona. The driver alleges that the "driver plus feature" was active and that the vehicle "veered sharply left," causing it to collide with the center median. The vehicle was subsequently towed from the scene. The driver reported visiting an urgent care facility. It is unknown whether he sustained injuries.
Based on available telematics, 33 seconds before the collision, the driver activated ACC. Then, 9 seconds before the collision, he activated Highway Assist. Next, 4 seconds after activating Highway Assist, the driver unbuckled his seatbelt while the vehicle was traveling 62 mph. The driver's actions triggered Highway Assist to disengage and issue audible and visual driver warnings. The driver immediately steered left, and the vehicle struck the center median. The vehicle was traveling at approximately 50 mph at the time of the crash.
Based on the information available, [XXX] believes the driver steering the vehicle left immediately before the impact caused the collision.

Narrative:
Customer contacted Kia via Web Note on June [XXX], 2025, and reported he was involved in an accident while driving his 2024MY Kia Sportage with Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) enabled at approximately 40-45 MPH.  Customer claimed that another vehicle was stopped at a traffic light and the subject vehicle did not slow down as expected.  Customer then attempted to disengage the ACC and manually apply the brakes, but the system failed to respond, preventing me from stopping the vehicle.  Customer added that the ACC system did not reduce speed appropriately or allow me to take manual control in [REDACTED, MAY CONTAIN CONFIDENTIAL BUSINESS INFORMATION] to avoid the crash.  Customer reported there was a significant collision, during which the airbags deployed.   He confirmed that he was able to drive the vehicle away from the scene.
Customer reported sustaining injuries to his thumb and stomach because of [REDACTED, MAY CONTAIN CONFIDENTIAL BUSINESS INFORMATION] seat belt and requested warranty coverage, a vehicle replacement, and compensation.  The customer confirmed they were not transported to the hospital and is not currently under any medical attention.
Kia Customer Care (CC) contacted customer to obtain additional information regarding the incident.  Customer reported that Highway Driving Assist (HDA), Smart Cruise Control (SCC) and Lane Following Assist (LFA) were also on at the time of the incident and provided post-incident photos of the vehicle.   The photos provided by the customer show damage to the center portion of the front bumper, consistent with contact against the rear tow hitch on the other vehicle which is also visible in the photos.  Kia escalated the case for further review. 
Kia confirmed that both SCC and LFA were engaged at the time of the crash.  While the customer reported that HDA was also active, Kia confirmed that HDA was not engaged at the time of the crash.  Kia also reviewed the ECU data which confirmed that the brake pedal was pressed two seconds before impact, which disengaged the SCC system.   The data also indicates that the Forward Collision-Avoidance Assist (FCA) system detected a vehicle ahead and issued a Level 1 audible and visual warning prior to the collision.  These data points suggest that the driver did not brake early or forcefully enough to avoid the collision.
The subject Kia Sportage is equipped with HDA, LFA, and Smart Cruise Control (SCC). Another name for SCC is Adaptive Cruise Control, which is how the customer refers to the system.  LFA is a supplemental system that helps to detect lane markings and/or vehicles on the road and assists the drivers steering to help keep the vehicle between lanes.  The functionality of LFA is contingent upon several outside factors and it is always the responsibility of the driver to safely steer the vehicle and to maintain the vehicle in its lane.
Further, post-impact drive cycle data shows no braking concerns.  The vehicle was driven away from the scene without reported braking issues, and the brake system continued to function as expected.  
Kia is currently working to obtain additional information to evaluate this incident.

On July 15, 2025, Kia received a request from Mr. Teitelman via email for materials relating to the incident report. On July 23, 2025, Kia provided Mr. Teitelman the requested information via NHTSA's SLFTS portal. In addition, the customer has since stated he is unable to provide any additional information because he sold the vehicle to his insurance provider. Kia has no further information.

Narrative:
The information contained in this report is based on Nissan's understanding of the allegations received during Claimant's discussions with Nissan's Consumer Affairs representatives and vehicle inspector, as well as results from Nissan's vehicle inspection.
On 6/28/24 claimant notified Nissan Consumer Affairs of an accident which reportedly occurred on 5/[XXX]/24. The claimant states that ProPilot Assist was in use and did not stop the vehicle. Claimant states he was "prevented from braking," explaining that the brakes had failed. Claimant reports neck pain and a small scratch on right thumb and finger, but did not seek medical attention. Claimant stated the complaint is related to brake failure and claimant is not reporting a failure of the ProPilot Assist performance.
Nissan inspected the subject vehicle in connection with the brake failure complaint and found the vehicle's braking system to be fully functional. No related DTCs were noted. AEB warning lamp was off, indicating a fully functional system. EDR data shows the vehicle was traveling at 64.6mph prior to the brake application applied 3 seconds before time zero. Vehicle data shows that prior to impact, vehicle speed had been reduced to below 20 mph, with AEB activating max braking 0.5 second before the crash.  Of particular note, the driver was issued a citation by police for following a vehicle too closely as he was approaching an intersection with a 25 deg right hand turn and the EDR system does not record the status of PPA system. According to the vehicle investigation report, the subject vehicle was towed from the incident scene.  
Nissan denied the claim because there is no evidence of any manufacturing or product defect that would have caused or contributed to this incident.
On July 22, 2025, during review of the vehicle inspection report for this incident in preparation of Nissan's response to EA25-001, Nissan determined that this incident was reportable under the criteria of the Second Amended Standing General Order 2021-01 and is therefore submitting this 5-day and Final Report.

The functionality of these systems is contingent upon several outside factors, and it is always the responsibility of the driver to safely steer the vehicle and to maintain the vehicle in its lane.
Kia is working to inspect the vehicle, but has no further information at this time.

Narrative:
The subject vehicle was traveling on [XXX] NB in Millcreek, UT. According to the customer's statement, the vehicle was traveling at 65 MPH with Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) and Lane Centering (LCF) engaged. The driver alleged that the vehicle's brakes were not operating and that after a mile, she had to crash into the highway median. The frontal and side airbags deployed, and the vehicle was towed from the scene.
Subaru inspected the subject vehicle on 6/24/2025. The vehicle sustained extensive damage to the front right side. The front right wheel, tire, axle housing, brake caliper, brake rotor, axle shaft, and strut were missing. Additionally, the front right brake hose and ABS wiring harness were severed. The front right side of the radiator support was pushed rearward, resulting in contact with the front timing cover. The impact caused the timing cover to fracture, exposing the camshaft sprockets. As a result of this damage, the engine could not be started.
[REDACTED, MAY CONTAIN CONFIDENTIAL BUSINESS INFORMATION]
Based on the vehicle inspection and the data acquired, Subaru determined that there was no abnormality with the vehicle braking system and that ADAS was not engaged within 30 seconds of the incident. The customer had manually cancelled ACC approximately 1 mile before the incident and had then likely mistakenly depressed the accelerator pedal rather than the brake pedal. The stability control system successfully reduced the vehicle's speed from 67.7 MPH to 37.9 MPH prior to impact by modulating engine power and activating ABS.

Narrative:
Customer contacted Kia via Web Note on June [XXX], 2025, and reported he was involved in an accident while driving his 2024MY Kia Sportage with Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) enabled at approximately 4045 MPH.  Customer claimed that another vehicle was stopped at a traffic light and the subject vehicle did not slow down as expected.  Customer then attempted to disengage the ACC and manually apply the brakes, but the system failed to respond, preventing me from stopping the vehicle.  Customer added that the ACC system did not reduce speed appropriately or allow me to take manual control in [REDACTED, MAY CONTAIN CONFIDENTIAL BUSINESS INFORMATION] to avoid the crash.  Customer reported there was a significant collision, during which the airbags deployed.  He confirmed that he was able to drive the vehicle away from the scene.
Customer reported sustaining injuries to his thumb and stomach because of [REDACTED, MAY CONTAIN CONFIDENTIAL BUSINESS INFORMATION] seat belt and requested warranty coverage, a vehicle replacement, and compensation.  The customer confirmed they were not transported to the hospital and is not currently under any medical attention.
Kia Customer Care (CC) contacted customer to obtain additional information regarding the incident.  Customer reported that Highway Driving Assist (HDA), Smart Cruise Control (SCC) and Lane Following Assist (LFA) were also on at the time of the incident and provided post-incident photos of the vehicle.  The photos provided by the customer show damage to the center portion of the front bumper, consistent with contact against the rear tow hitch on the other vehicle which is also visible in the photos.  Kia escalated the case for further review. 
Kia confirmed that both SCC and LFA were engaged at the time of the crash.  While the customer reported that HDA was also active, Kia confirmed that HDA was not engaged at the time of the crash.  Kia also reviewed the ECU data which confirmed that the brake pedal was pressed two seconds before impact, which disengaged the SCC system.  The data also indicates that the Forward Collision-Avoidance Assist (FCA) system detected a vehicle ahead and issued a Level 1 audible and visual warning prior to the collision.  These data points suggest that the driver did not brake early or forcefully enough to avoid the collision.
The subject Kia Sportage is equipped with HDA, LFA, and Smart Cruise Control (SCC). Another name for SCC is Adaptive Cruise Control, which is how the customer refers to the system.  LFA is a supplemental system that helps to detect lane markings and/or vehicles on the road and assists the drivers steering to help keep the vehicle between lanes.  The functionality of LFA is contingent upon several outside factors and it is always the responsibility of the driver to safely steer the vehicle and to maintain the vehicle in its lane.
Further, post-impact drive cycle data shows no braking concerns.  The vehicle was driven away from the scene without reported braking issues, and the brake system continued to function as expected.  
Kia is currently working to obtain additional information to evaluate this incident.

Narrative:
From original report (March 28, 2025):
Entries in this report are based on unconfirmed allegations:
On March [XXX], 2025, Toyota received an initial contact from the owner and driver of a vehicle involved in the crash. She described that cruise control was engaged leading up to the crash event, and alleged that she was in, "a single-vehicle accident due to an issue with the lane assist feature in her Toyota Camry veering the car hard to the right into a guardrail." She stated the resulted in the airbags deploying and total loss of the vehicle with  a tow-away from the scene. The driver alleged injuries including whiplash, concussion, and bruising as a result of the accident. The driver also stated she, "wants Toyota to be aware this is an issue, also thinks if it is lane assist Toyota should be partially responsible for getting into another vehicle of equal value and what she has already paid into the Camry on top of help with ER bills."
From 10-Day Update Report (April 7, 2025):
No materially new information is available at this time.
From Update Report (May 15, 2025): 
On April 17, 2025, Toyota completed vehicle inspection field report related to the crash event and obtained the Event Data Recorder (EDR) record. The record indicated that the vehicle's Dynamic Radar Cruise Control (DRCC) was engaged in the recorded moments leading up to the crash event. Toyota is investigating the Lane Tracing Assist (LTA) engagement status, along with the Pre-Collision System (PCS) activation status, and the timing of driver inputs related to steering, acceleration, and braking. This information will be provided in a future update. Minor revisions to information in the report fields above were made based on factual information obtained from the field report and EDR record.
From Update Report (June 15, 2025): 
Upon further review of the field report and EDR record for the crash event, Toyota confirmed that the vehicles Lane Tracing Assist (LTA) was not engaged in the recorded moments leading up to the crash event. During the approximately 4.5 second pre-crash recording, the driver did not engage the brakes. The accelerator pedal was at 0%, with the Dynamic Radar Cruise Control on and the vehicle travelling at a sustained 63.4 MPH. The steering angle was constant at 1.5 degrees until the impact event. The FCW and AEB were not triggered prior to contacting the guardrail.
